dublinCore.dc:title: 119 S3336 IS: Reliable Artificial Intelligence Research Act of 2025
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

form.current-chamber: IN THE SENATE OF THE UNITED STATES

action.action-desc: Ms. Hassan (for herself and Mr. Banks) introduced the following bill; which was read twice and referred to the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs

form.official-title: To require the Secretary of Homeland Security to carry out prize competitions to advance the science of interpretability and to develop adversarial robustness with respect to artificial intelligence products, and for other purposes.

section.text: This Act may be cited as the Reliable Artificial Intelligence Research Act of 2025.

paragraph.text: The term adversarial robustness means the degree to which an artificial intelligence model is able to resist attacks that would induce it to produce incorrect, restricted, or harmful outputs, while maintaining integrity, reliability, and privacy.
The term artificial intelligence has the meaning given the term in section 5002 of the National Artificial Intelligence Initiative Act of 2020 (15 U.S.C. 9401).

The term interpretability means the degree to which humans are able to accurately understand how an artificial intelligence model makes decisions and considers inputs and how the outputs or behaviors of the model respond to a change in the inputs.

The term red-teaming means a structured, interactive, and adversarial process to test an artificial intelligence system by simulating real-world actions to find vulnerabilities or flaws in the system.
The term Secretary means the Secretary of Homeland Security.

section.header: Prize competition for artificial intelligence interpretability research

subsection.text: Not later than 270 days after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary of Homeland Security shall commence carrying out at least 1 prize competition under section 24 of the Stevenson-Wydler Technology Innovation Act of 1980 (15 U.S.C. 3719) to advance the science of interpretability in a manner relevant to commercially available or widely used artificial intelligence products.
In carrying out the prize competition required by subsection (a), the Secretary shall consult with—

paragraph.text: the Secretary of Commerce;
the Director of the National Institute of Standards and Technology;
the National Cyber Director;
the Director of the National Science Foundation; and
any industry expert from the artificial intelligence sector in the United States that the Secretary considers relevant.
The Secretary shall develop the structure and evaluation criteria for a prize competition carried out under subsection (a) in accordance with the primary purpose described in that subsection.
The Secretary may—

subparagraph.text: structure a competition under subsection (a) into 1 or more phases, including submission of interpretability frameworks, submission of interpretable artificial intelligence models, and unique basic research; and
open these phases to the same, or to distinct, contestant pools.

paragraph.text: In developing the evaluation criteria for the frameworks, models, or methods submitted for a prize competition under subsection (a), the Secretary shall consider—

subparagraph.text: the degree to which a submission advances broadly applicable principles of artificial intelligence interpretability;
the practical value of a submission in making artificial intelligence more understandable and reliable in high-risk, high-value use cases; and
the likelihood that the unique research submitted will create standards for artificial intelligence interpretability in the government or industry.

subsection.text: The Secretary may enter into contracts, cooperative agreements, or other agreements with for-profit or nonprofit entities or State, territorial, local, or Tribal agencies to design and administer any prize competition carried out under subsection (a).

section.header: Prize competition for artificial intelligence adversarial robustness research

subsection.text: Not later than 270 days after the date of enactment of this Act, the Secretary shall commence carrying out at least 1 prize competition under section 24 of the Stevenson-Wydler Technology Innovation Act of 1980 (15 U.S.C. 3719) to develop capable artificial intelligence models that are designed to exhibit adversarial robustness in circumstances necessary for at least 1 high-impact, high-risk application in government or industry.
In carrying out a prize competition required by subsection (a), the Secretary shall consult with—

paragraph.text: the Secretary of Commerce;
the Director of the Institute of Standards and Technology;
the National Cyber Director;
the Director of the National Science Foundation;
any industry expert from the artificial intelligence sector in the United States that the Secretary considers relevant; and

the head of any Federal agency who has authority or expertise in a high-impact, high-risk application of artificial intelligence that could be an appropriate subject for a prize competition under subsection (a).
The Secretary shall develop the structure and evaluation criteria for a prize competition carried out under subsection (a) in accordance with the primary purpose described in that subsection.
The Secretary may—

subparagraph.text: structure a competition under subsection (a) into 1 or more phases, including submission of adversarial robustness frameworks, submission of artificial intelligence models, and red-teaming; and
open these phases to the same, or to distinct, contestant pools.

paragraph.text: In developing the evaluation criteria for the frameworks, models, or methods submitted for a prize competition under subsection (a), the Secretary shall consider—

subparagraph.text: the degree to which a submission advances broadly applicable principles of artificial intelligence robustness; and
the practical value of the submission in reducing the risk of adversarial attacks in high-risk, high-value use cases of artificial intelligence.

subsection.text: The Secretary may enter into contracts, cooperative agreements, or other agreements with for-profit or nonprofit entities or State, territorial, local, or Tribal agencies to design and administer any prize competition carried out under subsection (a).
Not later than 180 days after the date on which the first prize competition concludes, the Secretary shall submit to the appropriate congressional committees a report that includes—

paragraph.text: an evaluation of how the results of the competitions inform the fields of interpretability and adversarial robustness;
an assessment of any gaps in these fields identified by the Secretary over the course of the competitions; and
any suggested action that Congress should take to advance the fields of interpretability, adversarial robustness, and any related research.

subsection.text: In this section, the term appropriate congressional committees means—

paragraph.text: the Committee on Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs of the Senate; and
the Committee on Homeland Security of the House of Representatives.

section.text: There is authorized to be appropriated to the Secretary to carry out this section $10,000,000 for the period of fiscal years 2026 through 2030.
